name: Herman Miller
philosophy: An inspired interpretation of Herman Miller's brand language — the American modernist furniture house responsible for the Eames Lounge, the Aeron chair, and a century of design-museum-permanent-collection objects. Canvas is warm gallery-white (#faf9f6) or a deep editorial near-black for hero immersion. Type runs a thoughtful serif (Söhne Mono or proprietary modernist serif) at editorial scale paired with a precise grotesk for body. Photography is fine-art product-as-sculpture — single chair in a vast gallery space, side-light, no styling props. The voice is museum-curator: object name, year, designer, materials — written as exhibition wall labels, not as e-commerce descriptions.

design_language.motion_signature: Gallery-pacing. Hero images cross-fade slowly (~2s) — never quick. Product detail pages reveal product specs (designer, year, materials) with a slow 600ms cascade. Hover on product tiles fades a thin hairline border in over 300ms — no scale, no shadow lift. The brand voice is museum, not e-commerce.

trademark_signals: Gallery-white canvas (#faf9f6) with single product staged in vast negative space — Aeron chair occupying 30% of the viewport with 70% empty floor — the absence IS the editorial
Exhibition-label product captions — 'Aeron Chair, 1994, by Bill Stumpf and Don Chadwick' set in monospaced type beneath the photograph — museum, not catalog
Designer-credit type — every product page leads with the designer's name and the year of design, treating each chair as a historical artifact
Editorial long-form essays about chair manufacturing, materials science, and ergonomic research — written as cultural-magazine features, not as marketing copy
Single-image hero with low-saturation studio lighting — never multi-product collages, never lifestyle staging with people
ai_slop_avoided: Don't add lifestyle staging. Herman Miller photography is the single object on white floor in vast space — adding a person, a coffee mug, a rug breaks the gallery voice
Don't drop the designer credit. The product IS the designer's signature — removing the name reads as off-brand commercial
Don't increase product-tile density. The brand reads at 1-up or 2-up tile grids; 4-up reads as e-commerce, not museum
Don't introduce saturated colors. The brand is monochrome-on-gallery-white with the occasional warm-rust accent for upholstery shots; saturated CTAs read as off-brand
Don't shorten copy to marketing-bullet length. Product descriptions are full editorial paragraphs about the chair's history, materials, and design intent
